text: A 10 by 15 centimeter piece of iPOM mesh is visible, previously placed for umbilical ventral hernia repair.
The video shows two pieces of Progrip mesh in the left groin and a plug along with an unidentified piece of mesh in the right groin.
The left groin mesh plug overlay (MPO) is visibly blown out, while the right groin shows a scrotal hernia containing a large amount of small bowel.
The surgeon carefully reduces the small bowel from the scrotal hernia in the right groin, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon displays the mesh used for the bilateral inguinal hernia repair, highlighting its specific characteristics and placement within the surgical field.
The surgeon measures the surgical space, approximately thirty-six centimeters wide and eighteen centimeters tall, and selects a piece of barred mesh, characterized by its heavy weight, for placement.
The surgeon places a twelve millimeter port in the left lower quadrant to insert a large piece of mesh, which is cut to dimensions of thirty-six centimeters by eighteen centimeters.
The surgeon places a stiff, heavyweight mesh into the pelvic region, facilitated by the large exposure achieved through tap dissection.

The surgeon positions the heavyweight mesh to cover both MPOs and ensures it is tucked into the deep retropubic space. In the patient's right lower quadrant, the corner of the mesh appears slightly too long, indicating that the posterior lateral edge will be trimmed later.

The surgeon secures the mesh in the pelvic region using multiple 3-0 Vicryl sutures. Two sutures are placed on the left-sided Cooper's ligament, two on the right-sided Cooper's ligament, one on the midline pubis, and several along the upper border of the mesh.
The surgeon avoids placing sutures in the triangle of pain and triangle of doom, areas typically avoided to prevent nerve and vascular injury during mesh fixation.
The mesh is shown perfectly tucked into the retropubic space, positioned between the bladder and the suprapubic rami and pubis.

The surgeon trims the excess mesh in the posterior lateral area and removes the trimmed piece from the body. The remaining mesh is tucked neatly, providing excellent overlap posteriorly and laterally.
The surgeon applies fiber and glue to secure the large swath of mesh in both posterior lateral corners, ensuring the posterior edge remains flat and does not lift up due to the unsecured area.

The surgeon reconstructs the peritoneal flap, which appears damaged from the takedown of a large scleral hernia. The redundant hernia sac is visible inside the peritoneal cavity, and the surgeon proceeds to resect it to prevent potential entanglement with the small bowel.
The surgeon closes the toe port fascial defect using an O Vicryl suture from within the surgical site.
